refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., does safety-driven shutdown count as unavailability?), (3) which subsystems or failure modes dominate unavailability, and (4) the allocation of availability targets across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services domains. High safety criticality means that safety-driven shutdowns must be distinguished from reliability failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure modes, maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Planned Maintenance) / Total Time. Stratify by subsystem (Vehicle propulsion, Teleoperation link, Safety Services) to identify bottlenecks. Compare against customer-defined target and acceptance threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.98 availability (propulsion, steering, hydraulics, sensors).
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability (network link, command latency, video feed).
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability (hazard detection, emergency stop, safety alerts).
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring and predictive maintenance alerts to enable proactive intervention.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundancy and fault tolerance across all critical subsystems (Vehicle drivetrain, Teleoperation network, Safety Services sensors) with automated failover and health monitoring.
DP-B: Establish a preventive maintenance schedule based on component MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) and condition-based monitoring, with spare parts inventory and rapid replacement protocols.

DP-C: Design a hierarchical availability architecture: Vehicle provides mechanical availability, Teleoperation provides connectivity availability, Safety Services provides safety-critical availability; integrate via a central Availability Management Module that monitors, logs, and reports KPI.

DP-D: Implement a combination of DP-A (redundancy), DP-B (preventive maintenance), and DP-C (integrated monitoring) with explicit allocation of availability targets to each subsystem and continuous feedback loop.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered availability strategy combining (1) subsystem-level redundancy and fault tolerance (Vehicle drivetrain backup, Teleoperation dual-link failover, Safety Services sensor voting), (2) condition-based and preventive maintenance with predictive analytics, and (3) an integrated Availability Management System that monitors real-time KPI, logs failure modes, and triggers maintenance alerts. Availability targets are allocated: Vehicle ≥0.98, Teleoperation ≥0.99, Safety Services ≥0.99, with system-level target ≥0.97 achieved through series-parallel architecture analysis.

potential_sources: Coupling between Vehicle redundancy design and Teleoperation failover logic: both must coordinate to avoid cascading failures (e.g., if Vehicle backup activates while Teleoperation link is degraded, operator may lose control).
Coupling between Safety Services availability and Vehicle/Teleoperation: if Safety Services fails, the entire system must shut down safely, creating a hard dependency.
Coupling between maintenance scheduling and operational availability: planned maintenance windows must be coordinated across subsystems to minimize cumulative downtime.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is well-structured because it explicitly allocates availability targets to independent subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and integrates them via a central monitoring system. The series-parallel architecture (Safety Services in series, Vehicle and Teleoperation in parallel with failover) is logically sound. Coupling risks are manageable through interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking) and clear ownership boundaries.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Component MTBF data is available and reliable for all critical subsystems. Risk: If MTBF estimates are optimistic, actual availability will fall short.

Assumption: Redundancy and failover mechanisms can be implemented without introducing new failure modes (e.g., failover logic bugs). Risk: Complexity of redundancy may reduce overall availability if not carefully designed.

Assumption: Teleoperation network link can achieve ≥0.99 availability in typical worksite conditions (cellular, satellite, or private network). Risk: Network availability is often limited by infrastructure outside NGHE control.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance analytics can accurately forecast failures before they occur. Risk: False positives (unnecessary maintenance) or false negatives (missed failures) will impact availability and cost.

Assumption: Safety Services can be designed to fail safely without cascading to Vehicle or Teleoperation. Risk: If Safety Services shutdown triggers uncontrolled Vehicle shutdown, availability target may be unachievable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) the redundancy architecture (e.g., 1oo2, 2oo3 voting) for each subsystem, (2) the failover latency and impact on operator control, (3) the maintenance strategy (condition-based vs. time-based), (4) the network availability assumptions and fallback modes, and (5) the safety shutdown protocol and its impact on availability metrics. Consider separating 'availability' (system ready to operate) from 'safety' (system safe to operate) to avoid conflating reliability with safety.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates availability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the Availability Management System (real-time monitoring, KPI calculation, maintenance scheduling, predictive analytics) and the integration logic that coordinates redundancy, failover, and safety shutdown across subsystems. The module is responsible for end-to-end availability measurement and reporting to stakeholders (Site Manager, Project Manager).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time health and status data from Vehicle (propulsion, steering, hydraulics, sensors), Teleoperation (network link quality, latency, packet loss), and Safety Services (hazard detection, sensor status). Used to calculate availability KPI and trigger maintenance alerts.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends safety-critical alerts to Safety Services and operator when availability thresholds are breached or predictive maintenance is needed. Triggers emergency shutdown if critical subsystem availability drops below safe operating threshold.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational tasking and maintenance scheduling requests from Site Manager and Project Manager. Coordinates maintenance windows to minimize cumulative downtime.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with Teleoperation subsystem to monitor network availability and coordinate failover to backup link if primary link degrades.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the Availability Management System and KPI calculation. It has clear ownership of integration logic, monitoring, and reporting. Minimal diffusion: only the predictive maintenance analytics may be shared with Maintenance/Quality subsystem, but core availability logic is contained.

Vehicle owns its own subsystem availability (propulsion, steering, hydraulics) but does not own the system-level availability KPI or the integration with Teleoperation and Safety Services. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would diffuse responsibility for network and safety availability, violating separation of concerns.
Teleoperation owns network link availability but does not own Vehicle or Safety Services availability. Assigning DP-D to Teleoperation would create coupling and diffusion across subsystems.

Safety Services owns safety-critical availability (hazard detection, emergency stop) but is not responsible for Vehicle propulsion or Teleoperation network availability. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate safety with reliability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the correct owner of DP-D (integrated availability strategy) because: (1) it is the only module with visibility across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (2) it owns the system-level KPI and measurement responsibility, (3) it can coordinate redundancy, failover, and maintenance scheduling across subsystems without diffusion, and (4) it has clear interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) to communicate with subsystems and stakeholders.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it owns too much integration logic. Mitigation: Clearly separate concerns—NGHE Solution owns KPI calculation and monitoring, but delegates subsystem-level redundancy design to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Risk: Availability measurement depends on accurate Telemetry from all subsystems. If Telemetry interface is incomplete or unreliable, KPI will be inaccurate. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface contract explicitly (data types, update frequency, accuracy requirements).

Risk: Predictive maintenance analytics may require machine learning models that are difficult to validate and certify, especially in safety-critical context. Mitigation: Separate predictive analytics (lower assurance) from deterministic availability calculation (higher assurance).

Risk: Network Link availability is outside NGHE control if it depends on external infrastructure (cellular, satellite). Mitigation: Define network availability assumptions and fallback modes (e.g., degraded operation with reduced teleoperation range).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution module boundary to explicitly separate: (1) Availability Monitoring (real-time KPI calculation, logging), (2) Availability Management (maintenance scheduling, predictive analytics), and (3) Availability Integration (coordination of redundancy and failover across subsystems). Consider whether Availability Management should be owned by NGHE Solution or delegated to Maintenance/Quality subsystem. Define the Telemetry interface contract in detail (data types, update frequency, accuracy, completeness) to ensure KPI calculation is reliable.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layered availability strategy) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP is logically sound: it combines redundancy (fault tolerance), preventive maintenance (MTBF-based), and integrated monitoring (KPI calculation). The allocation of availability targets to subsystems (Vehicle ≥0.98, Teleoperation ≥0.99, Safety Services ≥0.99) is mathematically justified for series-parallel architecture. The DP is not over-constrained and allows flexibility in implementation (e.g., choice of redundancy architecture, maintenance strategy).

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Predictive maintenance analytics may be shared between NGHE Solution and Maintenance/Quality subsystem. Mitigation: Define clear ownership and interface contract for analytics models.

Diffusion Risk 2: Failover logic for Teleoperation may be implemented in both Teleoperation module and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Assign failover logic to Teleoperation, with NGHE Solution monitoring and triggering failover via SafetyAlert interface.

Diffusion Risk 3: Safety shutdown protocol may be implemented in both Safety Services and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Assign shutdown logic to Safety Services, with NGHE Solution monitoring and requesting shutdown via SafetyAlert interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary for DP-D because it owns system-level availability KPI and has clear interfaces to all subsystems. Subsystem-level redundancy and failover logic should be owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively, with NGHE Solution coordinating via well-defined interfaces. This avoids diffusion and maintains separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible via operational telemetry collection and KPI calculation. The NGHE Solution module can implement automated logging and reporting of availability metrics. Verification requires: (1) definition of measurement period and baseline, (2) clear definition of 'available' vs. 'unavailable' states, (3) stratification by subsystem to identify bottlenecks, and (4) comparison against customer-defined target. Verification is not dependent on detailed design of subsystems, only on their health/status telemetry.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-004 to NGHE Solution is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution respectively. Each sub-FR has a clear owner and measurable target. Allocation is consistent with system architecture and stakeholder responsibilities.

recommended_revisions: Refine the definition of 'available' vs. 'unavailable' to distinguish between: (1) unplanned downtime (reliability failure), (2) planned maintenance (scheduled downtime), and (3) safety-driven shutdown (safety action). Clarify whether each category counts toward or against the availability KPI.
Define the measurement period and baseline (e.g., 30-day rolling average, 1-year baseline) to enable consistent KPI calculation and trending.
Specify the network availability assumptions for Teleoperation (e.g., cellular coverage, latency, packet loss) and define fallback modes if network availability is lower than ≥0.99.

Define the interface contracts for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link in detail (data types, update frequency, accuracy, completeness) to ensure NGHE Solution can reliably calculate KPI.
Clarify the ownership and implementation of predictive maintenance analytics (NGHE Solution vs. Maintenance/Quality subsystem) and define the interface for maintenance scheduling.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into subsystem-level availability targets and implementation strategies. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is correct. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) require further decomposition to define the specific redundancy architectures, maintenance strategies, and monitoring mechanisms for each subsystem. Additionally, the interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) must be refined to ensure reliable KPI calculation. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to translate the high-level availability strategy into concrete design parameters and implementation modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.98 availability through redundant propulsion, steering, and hydraulic systems with automated failover and health monitoring.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability through dual-link failover (primary and backup network), low-latency command processing, and video feed redundancy.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability through sensor voting, redundant hazard detection, and fail-safe emergency stop mechanisms.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring via the Telemetry interface, with automated KPI calculation, failure mode logging, and predictive maintenance alerts via the SafetyAlert interface.

assumptions: Component MTBF data is available and reliable for all critical subsystems.
Redundancy and failover mechanisms can be implemented without introducing new failure modes.
Teleoperation network link can achieve ≥0.99 availability in typical worksite conditions.
Predictive maintenance analytics can accurately forecast failures before they occur.
Safety Services can be designed to fail safely without cascading to Vehicle or Teleoperation.
Availability measurement is independent of external factors (e.g., operator error, worksite conditions) and reflects only system reliability and maintainability.
The customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 1 year).
open_risks: Network availability risk: Teleoperation network link availability depends on external infrastructure (cellular, satellite, private network) that may not meet ≥0.99 target in all worksite conditions.
Complexity risk: Redundancy and failover mechanisms may introduce new failure modes or reduce overall availability if not carefully designed and tested.
Predictive maintenance risk: Analytics models may produce false positives (unnecessary maintenance) or false negatives (missed failures), impacting availability and cost.
Safety-reliability coupling risk: Safety shutdown protocol may conflict with availability target if safety-driven shutdowns are counted as unavailability.
Measurement risk: Availability KPI depends on accurate and complete Telemetry from all subsystems. If Telemetry is incomplete or unreliable, KPI will be inaccurate.
Maintenance coordination risk: Planned maintenance windows must be coordinated across subsystems to minimize cumulative downtime, but this may be difficult to achieve in practice.
evidence_needed: Component MTBF data and failure mode analysis for Vehicle propulsion, steering, hydraulics, and sensors.
Network availability data for Teleoperation link in typical worksite conditions (cellular, satellite, private network).
Redundancy architecture options and their impact on availability (e.g., 1oo2, 2oo3 voting, active-standby failover).
Failover latency and impact on operator control and safety.
Maintenance strategy options (condition-based vs. time-based) and their impact on availability and cost.
Predictive maintenance analytics models and their accuracy (precision, recall, false positive rate).
Safety shutdown protocol and its impact on availability metrics (e.g., does safety-driven shutdown count as unavailability?).
Telemetry interface specification (data types, update frequency, accuracy, completeness).
Customer-defined availability target and evaluation period.
Worksite conditions and network infrastructure assumptions.
